retrieved_docs.title: Fibric Acid Antilipemic Agents -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Cholelithiasis : Cholelithiasis may occur with fenofibrate and gemfibrozil due to increased cholesterol excretion into bile. If cholelithiasis is clinically suspected, diagnostic evaluation of the gallbladder is warranted. The discovery of gallstones should prompt the discontinuation of fibrate therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is well known that obesity is associated with a shortened life span and increased incidence of obstructive sleep apnea, osteoarthritis, gout, polycystic ovarian syndrome, metabolic syndrome, prediabetes, hypertension, stroke, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, heart failure, and cancers of the colon, breast, endometrium, and gallbladder. [7] Thus the treatment of obesity is essential, along with early identification of complications. Although this article focuses on non-dieting approaches to treating obesity with medications, gastrointestinal devices, and bariatric surgery, it must be emphasized that diet and exercise are essential to good cardiometabolic health in obese patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Nifedipine -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Post-marketing data have identified several risk factors for serious gastrointestinal obstruction in patients receiving nifedipine. [28] [40] Anatomical risk factors include gastric bypass, vertical banded gastroplasty, bowel resection, colostomy, diverticulosis, colon cancer, small bowel obstruction, inflammatory bowel disease, and strictures. Additional contributors include GI hypomotility disorders such as ileus, obesity, hypothyroidism, and diabetes, as well as concurrent use of opioids, anticholinergic agents, and neuromuscular blocking drugs.

retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both indicated for the treatment of gastrointestinal disturbances (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ). However, each of these bismuth compounds is contraindicated in patients who have atherosclerotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري), meaning they should not be used in individuals with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that includes the drugs colestipol and colesevelam, both of which are classified as bile acid sequestrants. While colestipol can be used broadly within this class, colesevelam carries a specific contraindication: it should not be prescribed to patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس. Recognizing this disease‑specific restriction is essential for safe selection of a bile acid sequestrant in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrate (fenofibrát) is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals whose kidney function is impaired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are classified as plain lipid‑modifying agents, a drug class used to lower cholesterol levels. Colesevelam is a specific medication that belongs to the bile acid sequestrant class. Because of its pharmacologic properties, colesevelam is contraindicated in patients who have الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس, and therefore should not be prescribed to individuals with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are classified as plain lipid‑modifying agents, placing them within the broader group of lipid modifying agents. Colesevelam is a specific bile acid sequestrant, and therefore also belongs to the plain lipid‑modifying agents category and to the overall lipid‑modifying agents class. The class “Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain” is likewise a subclass of plain lipid‑modifying agents and includes the individual agents bezafibrát and fenofibrát as members. Both the bile acid sequestrant class and the fibrate class are encompassed by the higher‑level category LIPID MODIFYING AGENTS. In addition, colesevelam is contraindicated in patients with the disease الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس, indicating that this lipid‑lowering therapy should be avoided in that condition. This integrated understanding assists clinicians in selecting appropriate lipid‑modifying therapies while recognizing specific contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1681
retrieved_docs.content: Dienogest should not be used in patients with coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي), as the medication is contraindicated in this condition due to the potential for adverse cardiovascular effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1980
retrieved_docs.content: Colesevelam, a bile‑acid sequestrant used to lower cholesterol levels, is contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis), indicating that this medication should not be prescribed to individuals suffering from this inflammatory pancreatic disease due to safety concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that function by binding bile acids in the intestine, thereby reducing cholesterol reabsorption and prompting the liver to convert more cholesterol into bile acids. Two commonly used members of this class are colestipol and colesevelam, both of which share the same mechanism of action and are employed to help manage hypercholesterolemia, particularly when statin therapy alone is insufficient or contraindicated. By decreasing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, these agents contribute to cardiovascular risk reduction and are an important therapeutic option in comprehensive dyslipidemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 686
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is a specific manifestation that has acute coronary syndrome as its parent condition and is further related to coronary heart disease (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ). Both acute coronary syndrome and coronary heart disease belong to the SMQ “Arterial Thrombosis and Embolism” (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية) and share the same coronary arterial location. Alirocumab is identified as a lipid‑lowering therapy that may be used to prevent coronary heart disease, linking treatment to risk reduction in this vascular domain. Clinically, acute coronary syndrome has been associated with bladder cancer (سرطان المثانة) and gout (مرض النقرس), indicating relevant comorbid relationships for patient assessment and management. Coronary atherosclerotic disease (تصلب عصيدي في التاجي) and coronary artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان التاجي) are also recognized within the coronary arteries. Coronary stenosis (strictuur; coronair) is specifically associated with the broader concept of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1637
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is linked to the condition described as احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ, which is classified within the broader group of حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ). This cardiac condition is therefore considered a member of the thrombotic and arterial embolism spectrum. Clinical evidence indicates that alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting lipid‑lowering therapy, can be employed to prevent the occurrence of احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ, highlighting its role in cardiovascular risk reduction. In addition to its cardiovascular relevance, احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ shows clinical associations with سرطان المثانة (bladder cancer) and مرض النقرس (gout), suggesting that patients with this cardiac condition may have an increased likelihood of these comorbid diseases or share common pathogenic pathways. Recognizing these interconnections assists clinicians in comprehensive risk assessment, preventive therapy selection, and coordinated management of patients who present with acute myocardial infarction and related systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate and fenofibrate are both classified as fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As members of the fibrate class, these agents are used to alter lipid metabolism, typically to lower triglyceride levels and raise high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol, thereby contributing to the management of dyslipidaemia. Their shared classification underscores their similar pharmacologic actions and therapeutic role in cardiovascular risk reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1679
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine, also known by the name isa, is classified as an anticholesteremic agent; it is used to lower cholesterol levels in patients, contributing to the management of dyslipidemia and associated cardiovascular risk.
